does: This paper presents AutoIF, a method for automatically generating high-quality instruction-following training data without manual annotation. The approach works by having LLMs generate instructions, corresponding verification code (unit tests) to check whether responses satisfy those instructions, and test samples to validate the code itself. Using execution feedback from running this code, the method performs rejection sampling to filter valid instruction-response pairs, producing data suitable for Supervised Fine-Tuning and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. AutoIF demonstrates consistent improvements across three training algorithms (SFT, Offline DPO, Online DPO) when applied to Qwen2 and LLaMA3 in both self-alignment and strong-to-weak distillation scenarios.
connects: This work builds on the longstanding problem of improving instruction-following in LLMs, extending prior research on data-centric approaches to model improvement and automated dataset construction. It connects to self-play methods that generate synthetic data for training, leveraging execution feedback similar to code-based evaluation approaches in recent work on program synthesis and reasoning tasks. The method synthesizes ideas from rejection sampling in language model training, verification-based feedback loops used in tool-using agents, and the broader movement toward reducing dependence on human annotation through self-supervision and automated quality assessment. It extends DPO and related preference-learning methods by providing a principled way to generate preference pairs automatically.
enables: This work opens pathways for scaling instruction-following improvements without proportional increases in human annotation effort, making high-quality instruction data generation more accessible for diverse domains and languages. It suggests broader applications of execution feedback beyond code—any domain where formal verification or automated checking is possible becomes amenable to this framework. Future work could extend the method to more complex, multi-step instructions where verification is harder, explore how to optimize the instruction generation and verification code jointly, and apply similar self-play-with-feedback loops to other capabilities like reasoning and planning. The approach also enables stronger models to efficiently improve weaker ones through distillation with automatically-verified instruction data.
